item.title: Crustal recycling and metamorphic dehydration govern the fertility of granite-associated tin systems
item.dc:creator: Xiang Sun
Huan-Chao Xu
Zhi-Ming Yang
Yong-Jun Lu
Ming-Jun Zheng
Run-Sheng Yin
Pete Hollings
Jin-Long Ma
Qing-Fei Wang
Jun Deng
item.description: Tin is a critical metal predominantly sourced from granite-associated deposits linked to anatexis of metasedimentary rocks and extreme magmatic differentiation. However, how metamorphism of sedimentary protoliths prior to crustal melting influences granite Sn fertility remains poorly constrained. Here we present an integrated boron (B) and mercury (Hg) isotope study of granites, metasedimentary basement rocks, and ores from the western Yunnan segment of the Southeast Asian Tin Belt. Tin granites and ores display Δ199Hg values of −0.41‰ to +0.06‰ and δ¹¹B values of −19.0‰ to −9.4‰, comparable to those of the basement rocks, indicating a dominant crustal volatile source. Coupled B–Hg isotopes further reveal variable volatile loss during metamorphic dehydration prior to melting, with partial melting of weakly dehydrated, volatile-rich metasedimentary protoliths favoring the generation of Sn-fertile granites. These findings highlight metamorphic preconditioning as a critical control on Sn metallogenesis and have important implications for global exploration of Sn resources. Integrated boron and mercury isotopes indicate that weakly dehydrated metasedimentary sources retain the volatiles needed to generate tin-fertile granites, highlighting metamorphic preconditioning as a key control on tin metallogenesis. Published: 2026-04-25T23:25:58+00:00 Venue: Communications Earth & Environment Score: 0.633 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiang Sun; Huan-Chao Xu; Zhi-Ming Yang; Yong-Jun Lu; Ming-Jun Zheng; Run-Sheng Yin; Pete Hollings; Jin-Long Ma; Qing-Fei Wang; Jun Deng

Journal: Communications Earth & Environment

DOI: 10.1038/s43247-026-03538-4

Relevance: 0.633 (must_read)

Tin is a critical metal predominantly sourced from granite-associated deposits linked to anatexis of metasedimentary rocks and extreme magmatic differentiation. However, how metamorphism of sedimentary protoliths prior to crustal melting influences granite Sn fertility remains poorly constrained. Here we present an integrated boron (B) and mercury (Hg) isotope study of granites, metasedimentary basement rocks, and ores from the western Yunnan segment of the Southeast Asian Tin Belt. Tin granites and ores display Δ199Hg values of −0.41‰ to +0.06‰ and δ¹¹B values of −19.0‰ to −9.4‰, comparable to those of the basement rocks, indicating a dominant crustal volatile source. Coupled B–Hg isotopes further reveal variable volatile loss during metamorphic dehydration prior to melting, with partial melting of weakly dehydrated, volatile-rich metasedimentary protoliths favoring the generation of Sn-fertile granites. These findings highlight metamorphic preconditioning as a critical control on Sn metallogenesis and have important implications for global exploration of Sn resources. Integrated boron and mercury isotopes indicate that weakly dehydrated metasedimentary sources retain the volatiles needed to generate tin-fertile granites, highlighting metamorphic preconditioning as a key control on tin metallogenesis.

item.title: Distinct mantle metasomatism in the mantle source of ultrapotassic magma revealed by Ca Ba isotopes
item.dc:creator: Yu Chen
Yi-Xiang Chen
Xiao-Feng Gu
Riccardo Avanzinelli
Sandro Conticelli
item.description: As powerful geochemical tracers, Ca and Ba isotopes provide complementary constraints on crustal recycling, mantle metasomatism, and crust-mantle interaction. However, the extent to which Ca isotopes can uniquely track recycled carbonate-bearing components remains debated, because light Ca isotopic compositions can also be produced by process effects ( e.g. , garnet-related fractionation) and/or mineralogical controls in hydrous metasomes. In contrast, Ba isotopes have emerged as a powerful tracer of crustal recycling because Ba is highly incompatible and shows limited isotope fractionation during high-temperature magma differentiation. Here we report whole-rock Ca and Ba isotope data, together with Sr Nd isotopes and major-trace element constraints, for a suite of Italian ultrapotassic volcanic rocks spanning silica-saturated to silica-undersaturated compositions. The samples show δ 138/134 Ba values varying from −0.11‰ to 0.07‰, and generally lower δ 44/40 Ca values (0.75 ± 0.08‰) than MORB on average, with one outlier (SVK 08) exhibiting an unusually low δ 44/40 Ca value (0.43‰). Petrographic and geochemical constraints indicate that low-temperature alteration, crustal contamination, diffusion, and shallow magmatic differentiation are unlikely to dominate the Ca Ba isotope compositions. Instead, the Ca Ba isotope variations are best interpreted to reflect heterogeneity in metasomatized mantle sources. For the silica-undersaturated rocks, low δ 44/40 Ca values are consistent with contributions from Ca-rich recycled components (including carbonate-bearing sediments), and two endmember mixing and Monte Carlo calculations provide plausible bounds on sediment addition. For the silica-oversaturated lamproites, comparably low δ 44/40 Ca values can be plausibly explained by melting of a K-richterite–bearing MARID metasome, while garnet-filtered sediment/mafic metasomatic melts remain a viable alternative for extreme values. Ba isotope compositions of the samples largely reflect sediment recycling but do not uniquely resolve carbonate-specific contributions. Overall, after accounting for garnet-related fractionation, Ca isotopes are most informative for the formation of Ca-rich, silica-undersaturated rocks, whereas Ca-poor lamproites remain non-unique and require evaluation with Ba–Sr–Nd isotopes and trace-element constraints. Published: 2026-04-25T15:02:41+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.631 (must_read)

Authors: Yu Chen; Yi-Xiang Chen; Xiao-Feng Gu; Riccardo Avanzinelli; Sandro Conticelli

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123452

Relevance: 0.631 (must_read)

As powerful geochemical tracers, Ca and Ba isotopes provide complementary constraints on crustal recycling, mantle metasomatism, and crust-mantle interaction. However, the extent to which Ca isotopes can uniquely track recycled carbonate-bearing components remains debated, because light Ca isotopic compositions can also be produced by process effects ( e.g. , garnet-related fractionation) and/or mineralogical controls in hydrous metasomes. In contrast, Ba isotopes have emerged as a powerful tracer of crustal recycling because Ba is highly incompatible and shows limited isotope fractionation during high-temperature magma differentiation. Here we report whole-rock Ca and Ba isotope data, together with Sr Nd isotopes and major-trace element constraints, for a suite of Italian ultrapotassic volcanic rocks spanning silica-saturated to silica-undersaturated compositions. The samples show δ 138/134 Ba values varying from −0.11‰ to 0.07‰, and generally lower δ 44/40 Ca values (0.75 ± 0.08‰) than MORB on average, with one outlier (SVK 08) exhibiting an unusually low δ 44/40 Ca value (0.43‰). Petrographic and geochemical constraints indicate that low-temperature alteration, crustal contamination, diffusion, and shallow magmatic differentiation are unlikely to dominate the Ca Ba isotope compositions. Instead, the Ca Ba isotope variations are best interpreted to reflect heterogeneity in metasomatized mantle sources. For the silica-undersaturated rocks, low δ 44/40 Ca values are consistent with contributions from Ca-rich recycled components (including carbonate-bearing sediments), and two endmember mixing and Monte Carlo calculations provide plausible bounds on sediment addition. For the silica-oversaturated lamproites, comparably low δ 44/40 Ca values can be plausibly explained by melting of a K-richterite–bearing MARID metasome, while garnet-filtered sediment/mafic metasomatic melts remain a viable alternative for extreme values. Ba isotope compositions of the samples largely reflect sediment recycling but do not uniquely resolve carbonate-specific contributions. Overall, after accounting for garnet-related fractionation, Ca isotopes are most informative for the formation of Ca-rich, silica-undersaturated rocks, whereas Ca-poor lamproites remain non-unique and require evaluation with Ba–Sr–Nd isotopes and trace-element constraints.

item.title: Formation and exhumation of the Yudai porphyry Cu deposit in the Kalatage region, Eastern Tianshan, NW China
item.dc:creator: Lin Gong
Huayong Chen
Zhiyong Zhang
Bing Xiao
Lin Wu
item.description: As the oldest recognized porphyry Cu system in the Chinese Eastern Tianshan orogen, the Yudai Cu deposit serves as a key example for investigating how ancient porphyry copper systems formed and were preserved in the Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB). However, its genesis and preservation remain poorly constrained because of conflicting interpretations of mineralization age, as well as limited knowledge of magma fertility and the exhumation history of the ore-related porphyries. In order to address these issues, we conducted a comprehensive investigation of the ore-related porphyries at the Yudai deposit, including diorite porphyry and quartz diorite porphyry, through zircon U–Pb-Hf and trace element analyses, apatite U Pb dating and trace element analyses, apatite fission-track and apatite (U Th)/He dating, and thermal history reconstruction. The results indicate that the Yudai deposit experienced at least three episodes of magmatic-hydrothermal activity, occurring at ca. 453 Ma, ca. 432 Ma, and ca. 391 Ma. The long-standing discrepancy in the reported ages of the ore-bearing porphyries is best explained by sampling of different porphyritic bodies. Although the Yudai magmatic system was characterized by relatively high magmatic H 2 O contents (~5.5–6.4 wt%), which favored porphyry Cu mineralization, its relatively low oxygen fugacity (ΔFMQ <0) likely limited its capacity to generate a large porphyry Cu deposit. The trace element characteristics of zircon and apatite further suggest that the diorite porphyry was comparably fertile to the ore-bearing quartz diorite porphyry. Thermal history modeling reveals differential exhumation during the Triassic (ca. 250–210 Ma) and Cretaceous (ca. 130–80 Ma), driven by fault activity. These results suggest that mineralization associated with the diorite porphyry north of the Yudai deposit may have been partly or largely removed by erosion. On this basis, future exploration should prioritize the southern and eastern parts of the Yudai district. Together with geological observations, we proposed that post-mineralization burial and later normal faulting were the main controls on the preservation of Yudai deposit. This study advances our understanding of the formation and preservation of ancient, overprinted porphyry Cu systems in the Eastern Tianshan orogen. Published: 2026-04-27T16:06:15+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.627 (must_read)

Authors: Lin Gong; Huayong Chen; Zhiyong Zhang; Bing Xiao; Lin Wu

Journal: Journal of Geochemical Exploration

DOI: 10.1016/j.gexplo.2026.108090

Relevance: 0.627 (must_read)

As the oldest recognized porphyry Cu system in the Chinese Eastern Tianshan orogen, the Yudai Cu deposit serves as a key example for investigating how ancient porphyry copper systems formed and were preserved in the Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB). However, its genesis and preservation remain poorly constrained because of conflicting interpretations of mineralization age, as well as limited knowledge of magma fertility and the exhumation history of the ore-related porphyries. In order to address these issues, we conducted a comprehensive investigation of the ore-related porphyries at the Yudai deposit, including diorite porphyry and quartz diorite porphyry, through zircon U–Pb-Hf and trace element analyses, apatite U Pb dating and trace element analyses, apatite fission-track and apatite (U Th)/He dating, and thermal history reconstruction. The results indicate that the Yudai deposit experienced at least three episodes of magmatic-hydrothermal activity, occurring at ca. 453 Ma, ca. 432 Ma, and ca. 391 Ma. The long-standing discrepancy in the reported ages of the ore-bearing porphyries is best explained by sampling of different porphyritic bodies. Although the Yudai magmatic system was characterized by relatively high magmatic H 2 O contents (~5.5–6.4 wt%), which favored porphyry Cu mineralization, its relatively low oxygen fugacity (ΔFMQ <0) likely limited its capacity to generate a large porphyry Cu deposit. The trace element characteristics of zircon and apatite further suggest that the diorite porphyry was comparably fertile to the ore-bearing quartz diorite porphyry. Thermal history modeling reveals differential exhumation during the Triassic (ca. 250–210 Ma) and Cretaceous (ca. 130–80 Ma), driven by fault activity. These results suggest that mineralization associated with the diorite porphyry north of the Yudai deposit may have been partly or largely removed by erosion. On this basis, future exploration should prioritize the southern and eastern parts of the Yudai district. Together with geological observations, we proposed that post-mineralization burial and later normal faulting were the main controls on the preservation of Yudai deposit. This study advances our understanding of the formation and preservation of ancient, overprinted porphyry Cu systems in the Eastern Tianshan orogen.

item.title: Origins of Miocene basaltic magmatism in the western Yarlung-Tsangpo Suture Zone, southern Tibet: Insight into the lateral and longitudinal tearing of the subducted Indian continental slab
item.dc:creator: Jianbo Cheng
Xianshu Dong
Yalin Li
Shuai Li
Siqi Xiao
Wenjun Bi
Rui Zhou
item.description: Post-collisional magmatic records (e.g., Oligocene–Miocene adakitic, potassic, ultrapotassic, and mafic rocks) in southern Tibet provide a unique insight into deep geodynamic processes beneath the Tibetan-Himalayan Orogen. However, the triggering mechanism and accompanied asthenosphere-lithosphere interaction for post-collisional magmatism still remain ambiguous. In this study, we present a combination of geological, petrological, geochronological, geochemical, and isotopic data for newly-identified Middle Miocene basaltic rocks in the Zhari and Kongmeda areas in the Yarlung-Tsangpo Suture Zone (YTSZ). The Zhari and Kongmeda basaltic rocks have a medium-K trachybasalt affinity and oceanic island basalt (OIB)-type geochemical characteristics with moderate La N /Yb N ratios (6.69–17.82 and 5.18–8.90) and slightly positive Nb–Ta–Ti anomalies. They also show relatively depleted Sr Nd isotopic compositions with initial 87 Sr/ 86 Sr (0.7058–0.7064, 0.7082–0.7092) and positive ε Nd (t) (+2.7 to +3.1, +1.2 to +2.4). Geochemical signatures and modeling results demonstrate that the Zhari and Kongmeda basaltic rocks were most likely generated by low-degree partial melting of asthenospheric mantle sources at or slightly below the ~75–85-km-deep garnet–spinel transition zone, along with minor involvements of lithospheric mantle components during magma ascent, implying asthenospheric upwelling and resultant asthenosphere-lithosphere interaction beneath the western YTSZ. Based on large-scale spatiotemporal analysis of post-collisional magmatic records in southern Tibet, the E–W-trending post-collisional magmatic zone with bidirectional center-ward younging trend was predominantly triggered by the bilateral tearing and detachment of the subducted Indian continental slab, along with minor effects from its longitudinal tearing, during the Oligocene–Miocene. Published: 2026-04-28T06:51:49+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.614 (consider)

Authors: Jianbo Cheng; Xianshu Dong; Yalin Li; Shuai Li; Siqi Xiao; Wenjun Bi; Rui Zhou

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123450

Relevance: 0.614 (consider)

Post-collisional magmatic records (e.g., Oligocene–Miocene adakitic, potassic, ultrapotassic, and mafic rocks) in southern Tibet provide a unique insight into deep geodynamic processes beneath the Tibetan-Himalayan Orogen. However, the triggering mechanism and accompanied asthenosphere-lithosphere interaction for post-collisional magmatism still remain ambiguous. In this study, we present a combination of geological, petrological, geochronological, geochemical, and isotopic data for newly-identified Middle Miocene basaltic rocks in the Zhari and Kongmeda areas in the Yarlung-Tsangpo Suture Zone (YTSZ). The Zhari and Kongmeda basaltic rocks have a medium-K trachybasalt affinity and oceanic island basalt (OIB)-type geochemical characteristics with moderate La N /Yb N ratios (6.69–17.82 and 5.18–8.90) and slightly positive Nb–Ta–Ti anomalies. They also show relatively depleted Sr Nd isotopic compositions with initial 87 Sr/ 86 Sr (0.7058–0.7064, 0.7082–0.7092) and positive ε Nd (t) (+2.7 to +3.1, +1.2 to +2.4). Geochemical signatures and modeling results demonstrate that the Zhari and Kongmeda basaltic rocks were most likely generated by low-degree partial melting of asthenospheric mantle sources at or slightly below the ~75–85-km-deep garnet–spinel transition zone, along with minor involvements of lithospheric mantle components during magma ascent, implying asthenospheric upwelling and resultant asthenosphere-lithosphere interaction beneath the western YTSZ. Based on large-scale spatiotemporal analysis of post-collisional magmatic records in southern Tibet, the E–W-trending post-collisional magmatic zone with bidirectional center-ward younging trend was predominantly triggered by the bilateral tearing and detachment of the subducted Indian continental slab, along with minor effects from its longitudinal tearing, during the Oligocene–Miocene.

item.title: Enigmatic postsubduction lavas from a fossil slab remnant beneath Baja California, Mexico
item.dc:creator: James K. Muller
Emily J. Chin
item.description: Geochemically unusual, low-volume lavas have erupted along the Baja California Peninsula (BCP) in Mexico since the Miocene. These rocks have remarkably high Sr contents and Sr/Y ratios. High-Sr/Y magmas are commonly ascribed to partial melting of subducting young oceanic crust (adakites) or melting of mantle previously metasomatized by slab melts (bajaites). However, BCP adakites and bajaites erupted after Farallon plate subduction ended locally. We leverage a novel approach to studying the Baja Californian adakites by combining phase equilibria and trace element modeling. Our calculated melts of a relict slab fragment (imaged geophysically beneath the BCP) are consistent with the trace element compositions of local adakites but only at high pressures when residues comprise garnet-bearing eclogite. We infer the high thermal regime needed to melt the fossil slab and overlying metasomatized mantle may originate via shear heating, sub-slab mantle flow, or a combination thereof. Recent studies have invoked slab melting to reconcile geochemical mass balance at active arcs. The high-Sr/Y BCP lavas may offer a unique, isolated view into this slab-derived component because dominant flux melting overwhelms such a signal in active subduction zones. Published: 2026-04-28T17:10:32+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.598 (consider)

Authors: James K. Muller; Emily J. Chin

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g53817.1

Relevance: 0.598 (consider)

Geochemically unusual, low-volume lavas have erupted along the Baja California Peninsula (BCP) in Mexico since the Miocene. These rocks have remarkably high Sr contents and Sr/Y ratios. High-Sr/Y magmas are commonly ascribed to partial melting of subducting young oceanic crust (adakites) or melting of mantle previously metasomatized by slab melts (bajaites). However, BCP adakites and bajaites erupted after Farallon plate subduction ended locally. We leverage a novel approach to studying the Baja Californian adakites by combining phase equilibria and trace element modeling. Our calculated melts of a relict slab fragment (imaged geophysically beneath the BCP) are consistent with the trace element compositions of local adakites but only at high pressures when residues comprise garnet-bearing eclogite. We infer the high thermal regime needed to melt the fossil slab and overlying metasomatized mantle may originate via shear heating, sub-slab mantle flow, or a combination thereof. Recent studies have invoked slab melting to reconcile geochemical mass balance at active arcs. The high-Sr/Y BCP lavas may offer a unique, isolated view into this slab-derived component because dominant flux melting overwhelms such a signal in active subduction zones.

item.title: Lithology of recycled sediments controls metasomatism in the lithospheric mantle: Examples from Italian ultrapotassic volcanics
item.dc:creator: Emil Akseli Koskinen Tonboe
Tod Earle Waight
Paul Martin Holm
item.description: Trace element and isotopic modelling indicate that the Cimini mantle source formed via interaction between peridotite and melts of subducted siliciclastic metasediment, producing phlogopite- and orthopyroxene rich (± accessory apatite and titanite) metasomatic veins. Stability at depth of accessory phases in the metasediment caused fractionation of trace element ratios such as Th/La and Sm/La in the vein rocks. Subsequent melting of the metasomatic veins produced the Cimini lavas. The Sabatini lavas formed from melts of subducted mixed silica and carbonate rich metasediment that reacted with peridotite and crystallized clinopyroxene and phlogopite. The compositional variation of Sabatini lavas reflects differences in metasediment proportions, melt-rock reaction, and immiscibility between carbonate and silicate melts. This study highlights the importance of recycled crustal lithologies in mantle metasomatism. Published: 2026-04-29T06:43:32+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.594 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Emil Akseli Koskinen Tonboe; Tod Earle Waight; Paul Martin Holm

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123453

Relevance: 0.594 (consider)

Trace element and isotopic modelling indicate that the Cimini mantle source formed via interaction between peridotite and melts of subducted siliciclastic metasediment, producing phlogopite- and orthopyroxene rich (± accessory apatite and titanite) metasomatic veins. Stability at depth of accessory phases in the metasediment caused fractionation of trace element ratios such as Th/La and Sm/La in the vein rocks. Subsequent melting of the metasomatic veins produced the Cimini lavas. The Sabatini lavas formed from melts of subducted mixed silica and carbonate rich metasediment that reacted with peridotite and crystallized clinopyroxene and phlogopite. The compositional variation of Sabatini lavas reflects differences in metasediment proportions, melt-rock reaction, and immiscibility between carbonate and silicate melts. This study highlights the importance of recycled crustal lithologies in mantle metasomatism.

item.title: Controls on Mg isotopic fractionation between deep mantle phases and relict signatures of a terrestrial magma ocean
item.dc:creator: Andrew M. Walker
Remco C. Hin
Tim Elliott
item.description: We use density functional theory to investigate the fractionation of Mg isotopes between phases in the lower mantle. Our results support previous work and show that coordination number plays an important role in controlling isotopic fractionation, with bridgmanite (perovskite-structured MgSiO 3 ) preferentially incorporating lighter Mg isotopes into its highly coordinated site compared to periclase (MgO). Increasing pressure enhances this fractionation while increasing temperature decreases it. These two effects trade-off such that the preference is evident across all lower mantle conditions explored, even to the high temperatures of the chondritic liquidus (e.g. Δ 26 / 24 " role="presentation"> Δ 26 / 24 Δ 26 / 24 Mg Per−Bdm " role="presentation"> Per−Bdm Per−Bdm is 0.04‰ at 4200 K and 87 GPa). In additional numerical experiments we separate the effect of coordination number from differences in bond length between different phases and these allow us to build an ionic model which parameterises magnesium isotope fractionation as a function of bond length, coordination number, and temperature. This model provides us with a preliminary means to describe isotope partitioning between solid and liquid phases when making predictions of Mg isotopic differences generated during terrestrial magma ocean crystallisation. We find that Mg isotopic fractionation between bridgmanite and melt, at lower mantle conditions, is sufficient to generate detectable differences in the Mg isotopic compositions of a residual melt or solid cumulate phase, relative to bulk Earth. More specifically, we show that isolation of a reservoir of cumulate bridgmanite that is some 3%–15% by mass of the mantle could account for the super-chondritic 26 Mg/ 24 Mg of accessible terrestrial peridotite samples. Whether such a reservoir can be dynamically preserved over Earth history remains an open question, but our results help quantify possible tests of such a scenario. Published: 2026-04-25T15:03:28+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.593 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Andrew M. Walker; Remco C. Hin; Tim Elliott

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.04.030

Relevance: 0.593 (consider)

We use density functional theory to investigate the fractionation of Mg isotopes between phases in the lower mantle. Our results support previous work and show that coordination number plays an important role in controlling isotopic fractionation, with bridgmanite (perovskite-structured MgSiO 3 ) preferentially incorporating lighter Mg isotopes into its highly coordinated site compared to periclase (MgO). Increasing pressure enhances this fractionation while increasing temperature decreases it. These two effects trade-off such that the preference is evident across all lower mantle conditions explored, even to the high temperatures of the chondritic liquidus (e.g. Δ 26 / 24 " role="presentation"> Δ 26 / 24 Δ 26 / 24 Mg Per−Bdm " role="presentation"> Per−Bdm Per−Bdm is 0.04‰ at 4200 K and 87 GPa). In additional numerical experiments we separate the effect of coordination number from differences in bond length between different phases and these allow us to build an ionic model which parameterises magnesium isotope fractionation as a function of bond length, coordination number, and temperature. This model provides us with a preliminary means to describe isotope partitioning between solid and liquid phases when making predictions of Mg isotopic differences generated during terrestrial magma ocean crystallisation. We find that Mg isotopic fractionation between bridgmanite and melt, at lower mantle conditions, is sufficient to generate detectable differences in the Mg isotopic compositions of a residual melt or solid cumulate phase, relative to bulk Earth. More specifically, we show that isolation of a reservoir of cumulate bridgmanite that is some 3%–15% by mass of the mantle could account for the super-chondritic 26 Mg/ 24 Mg of accessible terrestrial peridotite samples. Whether such a reservoir can be dynamically preserved over Earth history remains an open question, but our results help quantify possible tests of such a scenario.

item.title: A step metallogenic model and mechanism for hydrothermal vein-type gold deposits: A case study from the Jiaodong gold province
item.dc:creator: Mingchun Song
Zhiming Yang
Jie Li
Jian Li
Runsheng Wang
Chunyan He
Zhenliang Yang
Junjin Zhang
Tianci Xie
item.description: Hydrothermal mineralization systems and the geometric distribution of orebodies are structurally controlled. The step metallogenic model previously proposed, based on the structural ore-controlling patterns of the Jiaodong gold province, has provided strong support for breakthroughs in deep prospecting. However, this model still lacks systematic validation through deep observational data and does not adequately explain the formation mechanism of gently dipping, large, and thick orebodies. This study, through detailed statistical analysis of dip angle variations in orebodies and ore-controlling fractures from drill-hole data of typical Jiaodong gold deposits, as well as geophysical detection and inversion revealing deep fault structures and the coupling relationship between orebodies and faults, systematically reveals the ore-controlling patterns within gently dipping fault segments and the characteristics of stepwise mineralization. It indicates that gently dipping orebodies are the main contributors to the enormous gold endowment in Jiaodong Peninsula. Comparative analysis with other typical gold deposits in China suggests that large, thick, and high-grade gold orebodies are distributed in multiple layers from shallow to deep depths, constituting three forms of step metallogenic models: (i) orebodies hosted in the steep-to-gentle dip transition zones and the relatively gentle segments of the same fault; (ii) orebodies occurring in gently dipping secondary fractures adjacent to steeply dipping major faults; and (iii) orebodies controlled by gently dipping interlayer structures within strata bounded by multiple steeply dipping faults. Research indicates that rocks near gently dipping faults have weaker shear fracture resistance and are prone to stress concentration, while the faults themselves exhibit better sealing properties, which are conducive to fluid convergence and precipitation. Within the tectonic setting of the Jiaodong detachment fault system and with granitoids as the main host rocks, a fault dip angle of around 30° is most favorable for forming large ore-hosting spaces. The stepwise metallogenic model holds significant application value for deep gold exploration. Published: 2026-04-28T02:58:33+00:00 Venue: Science China Earth Sciences Score: 0.584 (consider)

Authors: Mingchun Song; Zhiming Yang; Jie Li; Jian Li; Runsheng Wang; Chunyan He; Zhenliang Yang; Junjin Zhang; Tianci Xie

Journal: Science China Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1007/s11430-025-1876-0

Relevance: 0.584 (consider)

Hydrothermal mineralization systems and the geometric distribution of orebodies are structurally controlled. The step metallogenic model previously proposed, based on the structural ore-controlling patterns of the Jiaodong gold province, has provided strong support for breakthroughs in deep prospecting. However, this model still lacks systematic validation through deep observational data and does not adequately explain the formation mechanism of gently dipping, large, and thick orebodies. This study, through detailed statistical analysis of dip angle variations in orebodies and ore-controlling fractures from drill-hole data of typical Jiaodong gold deposits, as well as geophysical detection and inversion revealing deep fault structures and the coupling relationship between orebodies and faults, systematically reveals the ore-controlling patterns within gently dipping fault segments and the characteristics of stepwise mineralization. It indicates that gently dipping orebodies are the main contributors to the enormous gold endowment in Jiaodong Peninsula. Comparative analysis with other typical gold deposits in China suggests that large, thick, and high-grade gold orebodies are distributed in multiple layers from shallow to deep depths, constituting three forms of step metallogenic models: (i) orebodies hosted in the steep-to-gentle dip transition zones and the relatively gentle segments of the same fault; (ii) orebodies occurring in gently dipping secondary fractures adjacent to steeply dipping major faults; and (iii) orebodies controlled by gently dipping interlayer structures within strata bounded by multiple steeply dipping faults. Research indicates that rocks near gently dipping faults have weaker shear fracture resistance and are prone to stress concentration, while the faults themselves exhibit better sealing properties, which are conducive to fluid convergence and precipitation. Within the tectonic setting of the Jiaodong detachment fault system and with granitoids as the main host rocks, a fault dip angle of around 30° is most favorable for forming large ore-hosting spaces. The stepwise metallogenic model holds significant application value for deep gold exploration.

item.title: The granite-pegmatite-associated Marvel Loch gold deposit, Yilgarn Craton, Western Australia: magnesian and calcic gold skarns in a 1.5 km long contact zone
item.dc:creator: Andreas G. Mueller
item.description: Marvel Loch (63 t Au) represents a group of high-P (300–400 MPa) gold deposits spatially associated with 2.65–2.62 Ga, garnet-muscovite and spodumene pegmatites in the 3.0 Ga Southern Cross greenstone belt, Yilgarn Craton. The ore bodies terminate at 650–800 m depth at pegmatitic granite. Structural relations, mass-balance calculations, 87Sr/86Sr and 206Pb/204Pb in scheelite and galena, and the δ13CPDB of CO2 (-4.8‰) in the calcite-depositing fluid implicate the I-type granite below as the source. Magnesian olivine-calcite-chlorite-phlogopite, quartz-diopside, and calcic diopside-hornblende skarn (4–15 g/t Au), and low-grade biotite-anorthite-microcline gneiss occur in a 1.5 km long foliated zone bound to the contact meta-komatiite / meta-gabbro. Garnet-biotite and Ti-in-biotite thermometry indicate a peak temperature of 650 –550 °C during calcic skarn and biotite-anorthite-microcline replacement in meta-gabbro, and cooling to about 400 °C during retrograde alteration of anorthite to phengite + prehnite + clinozoisite, confirming estimates for magnesian skarn. The fluid (log fO2 = -18 bar at 650 °C) became reduced during cooling and progressive replacement, as suggested by Fe3+/Fetotal ratios in whole rocks, CH4 in fluid inclusions, and positive Eu-anomalies in scheelite. At a sulfur fugacity of 10− 7-10− 9 bar, cooling is reflected in pyrrhotite + Ni-loellingite (600 –500 °C) in calcite-rich magnesian skarn, arsenopyrite + pyrrhotite + base-metal sulfides ± bismuth (550 –400 °C) in quartz-diopside skarn, and retrograde pyrrhotite + pyrite ± marcasite (450 –350 °C) in metasomatic gneiss. Marvel Loch is a gold skarn deposit formed at 11–15 km depth in a batholith environment. Reduced tungsten skarns (7–10 km) share the W-Au-As-Bi signature, and the reduction of a magmatic H2O-CO2 fluid during cooling and replacement of ferrous or graphitic host rocks. Published: 2026-04-28T02:26:59+00:00 Venue: Mineralium Deposita Score: 0.581 (consider)

Authors: Andreas G. Mueller

Journal: Mineralium Deposita

DOI: 10.1007/s00126-026-01448-1

Relevance: 0.581 (consider)

Marvel Loch (63 t Au) represents a group of high-P (300–400 MPa) gold deposits spatially associated with 2.65–2.62 Ga, garnet-muscovite and spodumene pegmatites in the 3.0 Ga Southern Cross greenstone belt, Yilgarn Craton. The ore bodies terminate at 650–800 m depth at pegmatitic granite. Structural relations, mass-balance calculations, 87Sr/86Sr and 206Pb/204Pb in scheelite and galena, and the δ13CPDB of CO2 (-4.8‰) in the calcite-depositing fluid implicate the I-type granite below as the source. Magnesian olivine-calcite-chlorite-phlogopite, quartz-diopside, and calcic diopside-hornblende skarn (4–15 g/t Au), and low-grade biotite-anorthite-microcline gneiss occur in a 1.5 km long foliated zone bound to the contact meta-komatiite / meta-gabbro. Garnet-biotite and Ti-in-biotite thermometry indicate a peak temperature of 650 –550 °C during calcic skarn and biotite-anorthite-microcline replacement in meta-gabbro, and cooling to about 400 °C during retrograde alteration of anorthite to phengite + prehnite + clinozoisite, confirming estimates for magnesian skarn. The fluid (log fO2 = -18 bar at 650 °C) became reduced during cooling and progressive replacement, as suggested by Fe3+/Fetotal ratios in whole rocks, CH4 in fluid inclusions, and positive Eu-anomalies in scheelite. At a sulfur fugacity of 10− 7-10− 9 bar, cooling is reflected in pyrrhotite + Ni-loellingite (600 –500 °C) in calcite-rich magnesian skarn, arsenopyrite + pyrrhotite + base-metal sulfides ± bismuth (550 –400 °C) in quartz-diopside skarn, and retrograde pyrrhotite + pyrite ± marcasite (450 –350 °C) in metasomatic gneiss. Marvel Loch is a gold skarn deposit formed at 11–15 km depth in a batholith environment. Reduced tungsten skarns (7–10 km) share the W-Au-As-Bi signature, and the reduction of a magmatic H2O-CO2 fluid during cooling and replacement of ferrous or graphitic host rocks.

item.title: Tracing deep Earth volatile heterogeneities with heavy noble gases in Réunion plume-influenced Central Indian Ridge basalts
item.dc:creator: Xinmu J. Zhang
Rita Parai
Peter H. Barry
Evelyn Füri
item.description: The initial budgets and subsequent exchange of volatiles ( e.g ., water, carbon, nitrogen, and noble gases) among surface and deep reservoirs govern planetary surface conditions and long-term habitability. Varieties of modern terrestrial basalts—mid-ocean ridge basalts (MORBs), which derive primarily from melting of Earth’s upper mantle, and plume-influenced basalts that sample the lower mantle—provide access to two distinct volatile reservoirs within Earth’s interior. Their heavy noble gas (Ne, Ar, Kr, and Xe) isotope systematics offer powerful insights into volatile accretion, outgassing, and recycling histories of different terrestrial mantle reservoirs. However, existing high-precision heavy noble gas datasets are limited due to challenges related to sample access and analytical methods. Basaltic glasses from the Central Indian Ridge (CIR), influenced by the nearby Réunion hotspot, provide an opportunity to expand the global heavy noble gas record. Here, we present high precision He, Ne, Ar, and Xe elemental and isotopic data measured from four submarine basalts collected from the CIR axis between 16.7°S and 20.6°S. The resulting heavy noble gas dataset confirms Réunion plume influence at the CIR axis near ∼20°S and provides constraints on the origins of the region’s compositional variations. The mantle source Xe composition of the Réunion-influenced CIR basalt D14–1 is consistent with observations from other plume-influenced localities, suggesting globally uniform plume-source volatile origins, with early-formed isotopic signatures preserved from accretion to the present-day. Published: 2026-04-28T12:55:52+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.577 (consider)

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120038

Relevance: 0.577 (consider)

The initial budgets and subsequent exchange of volatiles ( e.g ., water, carbon, nitrogen, and noble gases) among surface and deep reservoirs govern planetary surface conditions and long-term habitability. Varieties of modern terrestrial basalts—mid-ocean ridge basalts (MORBs), which derive primarily from melting of Earth’s upper mantle, and plume-influenced basalts that sample the lower mantle—provide access to two distinct volatile reservoirs within Earth’s interior. Their heavy noble gas (Ne, Ar, Kr, and Xe) isotope systematics offer powerful insights into volatile accretion, outgassing, and recycling histories of different terrestrial mantle reservoirs. However, existing high-precision heavy noble gas datasets are limited due to challenges related to sample access and analytical methods. Basaltic glasses from the Central Indian Ridge (CIR), influenced by the nearby Réunion hotspot, provide an opportunity to expand the global heavy noble gas record. Here, we present high precision He, Ne, Ar, and Xe elemental and isotopic data measured from four submarine basalts collected from the CIR axis between 16.7°S and 20.6°S. The resulting heavy noble gas dataset confirms Réunion plume influence at the CIR axis near ∼20°S and provides constraints on the origins of the region’s compositional variations. The mantle source Xe composition of the Réunion-influenced CIR basalt D14–1 is consistent with observations from other plume-influenced localities, suggesting globally uniform plume-source volatile origins, with early-formed isotopic signatures preserved from accretion to the present-day.

item.title: Quartz geochemical constraints on fluid evolution during low- to medium-temperature hydrothermal Sb and Au mineralization
item.dc:creator: Shen Gao
Xinyu Zou
Liangliang Huang
Hongye Feng
Jiajun Liu
Degao Zhai
Kezhang Qin
Guangming Li
item.description: Hydrothermal systems related to antimony (Sb) and gold (Au) ores form under low- to medium-temperature conditions (150−350 °C) and are often associated with major ore deposit belts. Ongoing debate surrounds the characteristics of ore-forming fluids, which are influenced by fluid sources, fluid-rock reactions, and evolutionary processes. Trace elements in quartz are critical for understanding fluid evolution in magmatic-hydrothermal systems (e.g., porphyry-epithermal deposits). This study analyzed trace elements in quartz from 43 Sb and Au deposits worldwide, focusing on low- to medium-temperature conditions. The deposits included Mesozoic Sb belts, Carlin-type and intrusion-related Au provinces in China, and late Paleozoic Sb-Au deposits in Europe. The findings reveal that Ti-Al geochemical diagrams indicate distinct affinities for the host rocks. Specifically, systems hosted by igneous rocks display elevated Ti/Al ratios (>0.005), whereas those hosted by sedimentary rocks exhibit lower ratios (<0.005). Metamorphic rock−hosted systems are best characterized by Sb-As plots and a ternary diagram involving (Ge + As)−Ti × 5−Sb. The observed relationships between Sb and As suggest that trends in Sb mineralization are more closely aligned with metamorphic rock−hosted systems, whereas trends in Au mineralization are associated with igneous rock−hosted systems in the current dataset. Sedimentary rock−hosted systems show quartz trace-element trends indicative of both Sb and Au mineralization. The Li-Al trends further highlight the distinct fluid evolution associated with Sb and Au mineralization, particularly in vein-type Sb, intrusion-related Au, and Carlin-type Au deposits. Variations in trace elements within quartz are attributed to compositional changes induced by cooling. Our results demonstrate that the trace-element signatures identified in quartz provide new insights into the evolution of fluids in low- to medium-temperature hydrothermal Sb and Au systems. Published: 2026-04-27T18:01:27+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.572 (consider)

Hydrothermal systems related to antimony (Sb) and gold (Au) ores form under low- to medium-temperature conditions (150−350 °C) and are often associated with major ore deposit belts. Ongoing debate surrounds the characteristics of ore-forming fluids, which are influenced by fluid sources, fluid-rock reactions, and evolutionary processes. Trace elements in quartz are critical for understanding fluid evolution in magmatic-hydrothermal systems (e.g., porphyry-epithermal deposits). This study analyzed trace elements in quartz from 43 Sb and Au deposits worldwide, focusing on low- to medium-temperature conditions. The deposits included Mesozoic Sb belts, Carlin-type and intrusion-related Au provinces in China, and late Paleozoic Sb-Au deposits in Europe. The findings reveal that Ti-Al geochemical diagrams indicate distinct affinities for the host rocks. Specifically, systems hosted by igneous rocks display elevated Ti/Al ratios (>0.005), whereas those hosted by sedimentary rocks exhibit lower ratios (<0.005). Sedimentary rock−hosted systems show quartz trace-element trends indicative of both Sb and Au mineralization. The Li-Al trends further highlight the distinct fluid evolution associated with Sb and Au mineralization, particularly in vein-type Sb, intrusion-related Au, and Carlin-type Au deposits. Variations in trace elements within quartz are attributed to compositional changes induced by cooling. Our results demonstrate that the trace-element signatures identified in quartz provide new insights into the evolution of fluids in low- to medium-temperature hydrothermal Sb and Au systems.

item.title: Hadean components preserved in Paleo- to Neoarchean rocks from the Yilgarn Craton, W-Australia
item.dc:creator: Max Hellers
Jonas Tusch
Carina Gerritzen
Eric Hasenstab-Dübler
Mario Fischer-Gödde
Andreas R.A. Schneider
Chris S. Marien
Josua J. Pakulla
R. Hugh Smithies
Martin J. Van Kranendonk
Dieter Garbe-Schönberg
Carsten Münker
item.description: The Narryer Terrane is known for its Hadean zircons, demonstrating the existence of evolved Hadean crust in the Yilgarn Craton (W-Australia). However, the effects of Hadean material on long- and short-lived isotope signatures have remained ambiguous. We present the first combined 146 Sm- 142 Nd and 182 Hf- 182 W dataset for rocks from the Yilgarn Craton to identify putative Hadean components in crustal and mantle reservoirs through time. We investigated these short-lived isotope systems in mafic-ultramafic sequences, metasediments and granitoids covering the oldest preserved Paleoarchean rocks up to the Neoarchean granitoids that mark the stabilisation of the craton. Our study reveals modern mantle-like 142 Nd/ 144 Nd in Paleo- to Neoarchean granitoids and Neoarchean komatiites, suggesting crustal and mantle sources that, at the time of extraction, had already homogenised isotope anomalies from Hadean Sm/Nd silicate fractionation events. We propose that slight µ 142 Nd variations between Williamstown Peridotite, Paringa Basalt, and Kambalda Komatiite samples from the Kalgoorlie Terrane resulted from assimilation of an ancient crustal component. Unlike 142 Nd patterns, many Yilgarn samples show clearly resolvable excesses in µ 182 W that range from +7.5 ± 3.0 to +10.3 ± 2.8 for Paleo- to Neoarchean granitoids and µ 182 W = +4.1 ± 0.8 (95% c.i., n = 11) for Neoarchean mafic-ultramafic sequences. The 182 W excesses likely record isotope signatures inherited from core-mantle differentiation and insufficient inmixing of late veneer material, preserved in the different mantle and crustal reservoirs related to the Yilgarn Craton. Overall, our results demonstrate that the Paleo- to Neoarchean mantle and crustal magma sources had been isotopically homogenised, with limited signatures from Hadean silicate differentiation but had not incorporated the complete late veneer budget of the modern mantle. Published: 2026-04-27T21:27:33+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.571 (consider)

Authors: Max Hellers; Jonas Tusch; Carina Gerritzen; Eric Hasenstab-Dübler; Mario Fischer-Gödde; Andreas R.A. Schneider; Chris S. Marien; Josua J. Pakulla; R. Hugh Smithies; Martin J. Van Kranendonk; Dieter Garbe-Schönberg; Carsten Münker

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120056

Relevance: 0.571 (consider)

The Narryer Terrane is known for its Hadean zircons, demonstrating the existence of evolved Hadean crust in the Yilgarn Craton (W-Australia). However, the effects of Hadean material on long- and short-lived isotope signatures have remained ambiguous. We present the first combined 146 Sm- 142 Nd and 182 Hf- 182 W dataset for rocks from the Yilgarn Craton to identify putative Hadean components in crustal and mantle reservoirs through time. We investigated these short-lived isotope systems in mafic-ultramafic sequences, metasediments and granitoids covering the oldest preserved Paleoarchean rocks up to the Neoarchean granitoids that mark the stabilisation of the craton. Our study reveals modern mantle-like 142 Nd/ 144 Nd in Paleo- to Neoarchean granitoids and Neoarchean komatiites, suggesting crustal and mantle sources that, at the time of extraction, had already homogenised isotope anomalies from Hadean Sm/Nd silicate fractionation events. We propose that slight µ 142 Nd variations between Williamstown Peridotite, Paringa Basalt, and Kambalda Komatiite samples from the Kalgoorlie Terrane resulted from assimilation of an ancient crustal component. Unlike 142 Nd patterns, many Yilgarn samples show clearly resolvable excesses in µ 182 W that range from +7.5 ± 3.0 to +10.3 ± 2.8 for Paleo- to Neoarchean granitoids and µ 182 W = +4.1 ± 0.8 (95% c.i., n = 11) for Neoarchean mafic-ultramafic sequences. The 182 W excesses likely record isotope signatures inherited from core-mantle differentiation and insufficient inmixing of late veneer material, preserved in the different mantle and crustal reservoirs related to the Yilgarn Craton. Overall, our results demonstrate that the Paleo- to Neoarchean mantle and crustal magma sources had been isotopically homogenised, with limited signatures from Hadean silicate differentiation but had not incorporated the complete late veneer budget of the modern mantle.

item.title: Petrogenesis of pegmatites in the Shakhdara−Alichur dome, South Pamir (Tajikistan): Constraints from apatite and monazite U-Pb geochronology, geochemistry, and Sm-Nd isotope analysis
item.dc:creator: Murodov Azamdzhon
Amjad Hussain
Sharifjon Odinaev
Davlatkhudzha Murodov
Sherzod Abdulov
Amirkhamza Murodov
Xin Ma
Ilhomjon Oimuhammadzoda
Safaralizoda Nosir Saidjalol
Wei Dan
Gong-Jian Tang
item.description: The petrogenesis of pegmatites in collisional orogens provides key insights into crustal melting and reworking. To investigate the age and source of pegmatites in the Shakhdara gneiss dome (South Pamir, Tajikistan), we integrated in situ apatite and monazite U-Pb geochronology, trace-element geochemistry, and Sm-Nd isotope analysis. U-Pb ages reveal pegmatite crystallization in the late Miocene (ca. 14−11 Ma for apatite; ca. 21−10 Ma for monazite) and host gneiss formation in the Eocene−Oligocene (ca. 35−27 Ma for apatite; ca. 28−22 Ma for monazite). The Nd isotopes show that pegmatite apatite [εNd(t) = −32.2 to +3.0] preserves signatures of reworked ancient crust, while monazite in both lithologies [εNd(t) = −20.8 to −4.2] reflects Nd equilibration during high-temperature anatexis. The F-rich, heavy rare earth element (HREE)-enriched, and Sr-depleted signature of the pegmatite apatite indicates crystallization from an evolved felsic melt. This melt was generated via anatexis of ancient basement crust, triggered by asthenospheric heating during lithospheric extension. In contrast, gneiss apatite is Cl-rich and light REE (LREE)-enriched, consistent with metamorphic recrystallization. Similarly, gneiss monazites are homogeneous and LREE-rich, contrasting with the chemically variable pegmatite monazites. Texturally, pegmatite minerals preserve euhedral forms, whereas gneiss grains show embayed margins and patchy zoning indicative of fluid-assisted recrystallization. Collectively, these features define a two-stage, genetically linked model for REE enrichment. Stage A (ca. 12.9−11.0 Ma) involved magmatic differentiation, which enriched the pegmatites in REEs and homogenized the isotopic signatures of the host gneiss. Stage B (ca. 11−10 Ma) featured hydrothermal overprinting by resolved fluids, driving the dissolution−reprecipitation of apatite and monazite. This magmatic−hydrothermal continuum confirms a common anatectic source. While apatite records signatures from ancient sources and fluids, monazite reflects isotopic homogenization, documenting the multistage reworking of the crust of an evolving collisional orogen. This study provides critical constraints on the petrogenesis and mineralization potential of pegmatite−gneiss systems in collisional orogens worldwide. Published: 2026-04-27T18:01:36+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.566 (consider)

Authors: Murodov Azamdzhon; Amjad Hussain; Sharifjon Odinaev; Davlatkhudzha Murodov; Sherzod Abdulov; Amirkhamza Murodov; Xin Ma; Ilhomjon Oimuhammadzoda; Safaralizoda Nosir Saidjalol; Wei Dan; Gong-Jian Tang

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b39007.1

Relevance: 0.566 (consider)

The petrogenesis of pegmatites in collisional orogens provides key insights into crustal melting and reworking. To investigate the age and source of pegmatites in the Shakhdara gneiss dome (South Pamir, Tajikistan), we integrated in situ apatite and monazite U-Pb geochronology, trace-element geochemistry, and Sm-Nd isotope analysis. U-Pb ages reveal pegmatite crystallization in the late Miocene (ca. 14−11 Ma for apatite; ca. 21−10 Ma for monazite) and host gneiss formation in the Eocene−Oligocene (ca. 35−27 Ma for apatite; ca. 28−22 Ma for monazite). The Nd isotopes show that pegmatite apatite [εNd(t) = −32.2 to +3.0] preserves signatures of reworked ancient crust, while monazite in both lithologies [εNd(t) = −20.8 to −4.2] reflects Nd equilibration during high-temperature anatexis. The F-rich, heavy rare earth element (HREE)-enriched, and Sr-depleted signature of the pegmatite apatite indicates crystallization from an evolved felsic melt. This melt was generated via anatexis of ancient basement crust, triggered by asthenospheric heating during lithospheric extension. In contrast, gneiss apatite is Cl-rich and light REE (LREE)-enriched, consistent with metamorphic recrystallization. Similarly, gneiss monazites are homogeneous and LREE-rich, contrasting with the chemically variable pegmatite monazites. Texturally, pegmatite minerals preserve euhedral forms, whereas gneiss grains show embayed margins and patchy zoning indicative of fluid-assisted recrystallization. Collectively, these features define a two-stage, genetically linked model for REE enrichment. Stage A (ca. 12.9−11.0 Ma) involved magmatic differentiation, which enriched the pegmatites in REEs and homogenized the isotopic signatures of the host gneiss. Stage B (ca. 11−10 Ma) featured hydrothermal overprinting by resolved fluids, driving the dissolution−reprecipitation of apatite and monazite. This magmatic−hydrothermal continuum confirms a common anatectic source. While apatite records signatures from ancient sources and fluids, monazite reflects isotopic homogenization, documenting the multistage reworking of the crust of an evolving collisional orogen. This study provides critical constraints on the petrogenesis and mineralization potential of pegmatite−gneiss systems in collisional orogens worldwide.

item.title: Long-lived (&gt;20 m.y.) prograde heating in the Eastern Himalaya in response to Neo-Tethys slab breakoff
item.dc:creator: Shuaiqi Liu
Guibin Zhang
Simin Huang
Mingcheng Duan
Lu Xiong
Shuzhen Wang
item.description: The Himalaya is the most prominent collisional belt on Earth, characterized by pronounced spatiotemporal variations in thermal gradients. The Western Himalayan syntaxis (WHS) records evidence of deep subduction with low thermal gradients, whereas the Eastern Himalaya is characterized by synchronous high-temperature granulite overprinting. The geodynamic mechanism responsible for these along-strike thermo-metamorphic differences remains poorly constrained, particularly regarding the timing and duration of heating during the India-Asia collision. This study investigates high-pressure pelitic granulites from the Eastern Himalayan syntaxis (EHS) to constrain their metamorphic evolution and duration of metamorphic stages. Pelitic granulites record a clockwise isothermal decompression pressure-temperature (P−T) path with peak conditions of 12−13 kbar and 815−830 °C, accompanied by muscovite-biotite dehydration melting. Metamorphic ages range from 47.3 Ma to 12.6 Ma, with long-lived prograde heating spanning from 47.3 Ma to 23.0 Ma. During this interval, zircon and monazite show increasing Y contents and decreasing (Dy/Yb)N ratios with decreasing age due to the breakdown of accessory minerals (e.g., xenotime and apatite). In contrast, retrograde metamorphism occurs at ca. 23.0−12.6 Ma and shows a reverse trend. The EHS thus documents prolonged (>20 m.y.) prograde heating, contrasting with the rapid subduction-exhumation in the WHS. Integrating the presence of Eocene magmatism, different P−T−time paths, and metamorphic ages between the EHS and WHS, this study proposes that the along-strike metamorphic differences reflect the evolving dynamics of the subduction slab system. The Neo-Tethys breakoff occurs earlier (ca. 47−45 Ma) in the EHS, thus resulting in flat subduction of the Indian lithosphere, Eocene magmatic flare-up, and long-lived prograde heating in the EHS. Conversely, the WHS experienced continuous continental subduction, with Neo-Tethyan breakoff occurring later (ca. 35 Ma) during the transition from subduction to collision. Published: 2026-04-27T18:01:30+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.566 (consider)

The Western Himalayan syntaxis (WHS) records evidence of deep subduction with low thermal gradients, whereas the Eastern Himalaya is characterized by synchronous high-temperature granulite overprinting. The geodynamic mechanism responsible for these along-strike thermo-metamorphic differences remains poorly constrained, particularly regarding the timing and duration of heating during the India-Asia collision. This study investigates high-pressure pelitic granulites from the Eastern Himalayan syntaxis (EHS) to constrain their metamorphic evolution and duration of metamorphic stages. Pelitic granulites record a clockwise isothermal decompression pressure-temperature (P−T) path with peak conditions of 12−13 kbar and 815−830 °C, accompanied by muscovite-biotite dehydration melting. The Neo-Tethys breakoff occurs earlier (ca. 47−45 Ma) in the EHS, thus resulting in flat subduction of the Indian lithosphere, Eocene magmatic flare-up, and long-lived prograde heating in the EHS. Conversely, the WHS experienced continuous continental subduction, with Neo-Tethyan breakoff occurring later (ca. 35 Ma) during the transition from subduction to collision.

item.title: A Common Mantle Source for the Endogenous CO <sub>2</sub> Degassed at the Eifel (Western Germany) and the Ardennes (Eastern Belgium) Regions
item.dc:creator: Lisa Ricci
Francesco Frondini
Daniele Morgavi
Stefano Caliro
Andrea Luca Rizzo
Tobias P. Fischer
Giovanni Chiodini
item.description: Abstract Tectonic CO 2 Earth degassing is globally relevant and has probably controlled climate on a geological scale (Brune et al., 2019, https://doi.org/10.1038/s41561‐017‐0003‐6 ). Endogenous CO 2 outgassing from rifting areas remains poorly constrained, with most available data from the East African Rift. Here, we investigate the CO 2 degassing in the European Cenozoic Rift System (ECRIS), focusing on the chemical and isotopic composition of 161 springs emerging from the Eifel‐Ardennes‐Rhenish Massif region (Eastern Belgium‐Western Germany). Theoretical water‐gas‐rock interaction models based on the chemistry of gases dissolved in East Eifel groundwaters reveal CO 2 efflux takes place in a P ‐ T range between the aquifer depth (4 bar, 160°C) and spring emergence (1 bar, 9°C). He and C isotopes show that Ardennes, Volcanic Eifel and Rhenish Massif are all part of a unique degassing system. The average mantle CO 2 flux emitted from the region is 5 ± 2 × 10 6 mol yr −1 km −2 , corresponding to a total deeply derived CO 2 emission rate of 7 ± 4 × 10 9 mol yr −1 . These values are of the same magnitude as the global baseline defined for convective hydrothermal CO 2 emitted from areas of high heat flow, demonstrating the relevance of passive rifts as CO 2 emitters on a global scale. Published: 2026-04-26T07:39:56+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.565 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lisa Ricci; Francesco Frondini; Daniele Morgavi; Stefano Caliro; Andrea Luca Rizzo; Tobias P. Fischer; Giovanni Chiodini

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012735

Relevance: 0.565 (consider)

Abstract Tectonic CO 2 Earth degassing is globally relevant and has probably controlled climate on a geological scale (Brune et al., 2019, https://doi.org/10.1038/s41561‐017‐0003‐6 ). Endogenous CO 2 outgassing from rifting areas remains poorly constrained, with most available data from the East African Rift. Here, we investigate the CO 2 degassing in the European Cenozoic Rift System (ECRIS), focusing on the chemical and isotopic composition of 161 springs emerging from the Eifel‐Ardennes‐Rhenish Massif region (Eastern Belgium‐Western Germany). Theoretical water‐gas‐rock interaction models based on the chemistry of gases dissolved in East Eifel groundwaters reveal CO 2 efflux takes place in a P ‐ T range between the aquifer depth (4 bar, 160°C) and spring emergence (1 bar, 9°C). He and C isotopes show that Ardennes, Volcanic Eifel and Rhenish Massif are all part of a unique degassing system. The average mantle CO 2 flux emitted from the region is 5 ± 2 × 10 6 mol yr −1 km −2 , corresponding to a total deeply derived CO 2 emission rate of 7 ± 4 × 10 9 mol yr −1 . These values are of the same magnitude as the global baseline defined for convective hydrothermal CO 2 emitted from areas of high heat flow, demonstrating the relevance of passive rifts as CO 2 emitters on a global scale.

item.title: Zinc isotope insights into the Archean continental crust stabilization: Evidence from potassic granites, Yilgarn and Pilbara cratons
item.dc:creator: Jianfeng Ma
Hamed Gamaleldien
Yuan-Ru Qu
Robert H. Smithies
item.description: Potassic granites are a major component of the Archean continental crust, yet their origin remains contentious due to uncertainties in source composition and melting conditions. We report integrated whole-rock geochemistry, Zn isotopes (expressed as δ 66 Zn relative to JMC-Lyon), and phase equilibria modeling for 3.22–2.65 billion years ago (Ga) potassic granites from the Yilgarn and Pilbara cratons, Western Australia. These granites show elevated K 2 O/Na 2 O (0.81–1.2) and variable δ 66 Zn (0.33 ± 0.03‰ to 0.50 ± 0.03‰, 2SD), overlapping those from the Kaapvaal Craton (δ 66 Zn from 0.30‰ to 0.46‰). Thermodynamic modeling indicates contrasting origins: Yilgarn granites formed by ∼10% partial melting of primitive tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) crust (K 2 O/Na 2 O ≤ 0.35, mantle-like zircon O isotopes), whereas Pilbara and Kaapvaal granites derive from 20 to 35% melting of evolved TTG crust (0.35 2 O/Na 2 O 18 O > 6‰). Heavy δ 66 Zn values (> 0.40‰) in a subset of potassic granites, especially where coupled with high Mg # (>40) and relatively low K 2 O/Na 2 O (<0.85), are most consistently explained by contribution from a carbonate-altered mafic source (e.g., carbonate alteration of oceanic crust), followed by 10–20% melting at 2–12 kbar and 640–850 °C. We propose that variations in source composition and partial melting conditions can account for much of the geochemical–isotopic diversity in the studied Archean potassic granites and identify Zn isotopes as a tracer of deep crustal recycling during early Earth continental stabilization and maturation. Published: 2026-04-28T15:30:34+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.563 (consider)

Potassic granites are a major component of the Archean continental crust, yet their origin remains contentious due to uncertainties in source composition and melting conditions. We report integrated whole-rock geochemistry, Zn isotopes (expressed as δ 66 Zn relative to JMC-Lyon), and phase equilibria modeling for 3.22–2.65 billion years ago (Ga) potassic granites from the Yilgarn and Pilbara cratons, Western Australia. These granites show elevated K 2 O/Na 2 O (0.81–1.2) and variable δ 66 Zn (0.33 ± 0.03‰ to 0.50 ± 0.03‰, 2SD), overlapping those from the Kaapvaal Craton (δ 66 Zn from 0.30‰ to 0.46‰). Thermodynamic modeling indicates contrasting origins: Yilgarn granites formed by ∼10% partial melting of primitive tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) crust (K 2 O/Na 2 O ≤ 0.35, mantle-like zircon O isotopes), whereas Pilbara and Kaapvaal granites derive from 20 to 35% melting of evolved TTG crust (0.35 2 O/Na 2 O 18 O > 6‰). Heavy δ 66 Zn values (> 0.40‰) in a subset of potassic granites, especially where coupled with high Mg # (>40) and relatively low K 2 O/Na 2 O (<0.85), are most consistently explained by contribution from a carbonate-altered mafic source (e.g., carbonate alteration of oceanic crust), followed by 10–20% melting at 2–12 kbar and 640–850 °C. We propose that variations in source composition and partial melting conditions can account for much of the geochemical–isotopic diversity in the studied Archean potassic granites and identify Zn isotopes as a tracer of deep crustal recycling during early Earth continental stabilization and maturation.

item.title: Weak asthenosphere of Sumatran backarc revealed by long postseismic geodetic records
item.dc:creator: Grace Ng
Lujia Feng
Xin Zhou
Haipeng Luo
Kelin Wang
Tianhaozhe Sun
Chien Zheng Yong
Emma M. Hill
item.description: Abstract The viscosity of Earth’s asthenosphere regulates plate motion and surface deformation. Assessing asthenosphere rheology in subduction-zone backarcs is as crucial as in forearc and arc regions for understanding subduction dynamics. High heat flow and low seismic velocities in backarcs suggest a low viscosity, yet its geodetic evidence remains elusive. Here, we leverage 12–20 years of geodetic observations from Thailand, Malaysia, and Singapore to provide the evidence. We observe sustained far-field postseismic motion following great earthquakes in the Sumatran subduction zone and Wharton Basin extending over 600 km from the ruptures. Using layered spherical Earth models, we demonstrate this deformation requires a weak asthenosphere with viscosity on the order of ~10 18 Pa s, with or without an even weaker lithosphere-asthenosphere boundary. These findings lend support to the conceptual model of buoyancy-driven small-scale thermal convection in subduction-zone backarcs. The extensive postseismic deformation due to the weak asthenosphere also affects sea-level projections. Published: 2026-04-29T01:28:16+00:00 Venue: Communications Earth & Environment Score: 0.557 (consider)

Authors: Grace Ng; Lujia Feng; Xin Zhou; Haipeng Luo; Kelin Wang; Tianhaozhe Sun; Chien Zheng Yong; Emma M. Hill

Journal: Communications Earth & Environment

DOI: 10.1038/s43247-026-03561-5

Relevance: 0.557 (consider)

Abstract The viscosity of Earth’s asthenosphere regulates plate motion and surface deformation. Assessing asthenosphere rheology in subduction-zone backarcs is as crucial as in forearc and arc regions for understanding subduction dynamics. High heat flow and low seismic velocities in backarcs suggest a low viscosity, yet its geodetic evidence remains elusive. Here, we leverage 12–20 years of geodetic observations from Thailand, Malaysia, and Singapore to provide the evidence. We observe sustained far-field postseismic motion following great earthquakes in the Sumatran subduction zone and Wharton Basin extending over 600 km from the ruptures. Using layered spherical Earth models, we demonstrate this deformation requires a weak asthenosphere with viscosity on the order of ~10 18 Pa s, with or without an even weaker lithosphere-asthenosphere boundary. These findings lend support to the conceptual model of buoyancy-driven small-scale thermal convection in subduction-zone backarcs. The extensive postseismic deformation due to the weak asthenosphere also affects sea-level projections.

item.title: Hafnium and silicon isotope evidence for multiple unsilicified basaltic sources at the origin of Rhyacian TTG magmas in the Mineiro Belt (Brazil)
item.dc:creator: Syro Lacerda
Martin Guitreau
Leonardo Gonçalves
Hugo Moreira
Cristiane C. Gonçalves
Stéphany Rodrigues Lopes
Sandro Mauri
Marco Aurélio Pinheiro
Cassiano Costa e Castro
Régis Doucelance
item.description: Tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite (TTGs) are silica-rich plutonic rocks that correspond to the dominant lithologies of Archean basements. Understanding their formation is essential to unravel the early Earth tectonic regime and constrain continental crust formation and evolution. Archean TTGs commonly exhibit heavy-silicon isotope compositions attributed to ocean-derived silica in their sources, thereby indicating an interaction with the surface. The sedimentary record shows that the dissolved silica in seawater (DSi) has decreased secularly from the Archean onward. Despite the general disappearance of TTGs after the Archean, some granitoids of TTG affinity can still be found in Paleoproterozoic crustal domains that are tectonically well-constrained. Comparing the geochemical characteristics of Archean and Proterozoic TTGs, as well as their sources, therefore appears important for feeding debates on the evolution of continental crust and tectonic settings. To shed new light on these questions, the present study examines the magmatic record of three Paleoproterozoic TTG units in the Mineiro Belt (Brazil) by focusing on whole-rock petrography and geochemistry alongside zircon U-Pb geochronology, elemental, and isotopic (Hf and Si) geochemistry. These new data indicate episodic formation of TTG magmas during the Rhyacian (2.15–2.10 Ga), mainly driven by melting of Rb- and Ba-enriched low-K mafic sources with variably long crustal residence times (∼0.2–0.8 Ga). Studied Paleoproterozoic TTGs exhibit lower silicon isotopic signatures than their Archean counterparts. This difference is likely linked to variations in source pre-melting histories, such that they may not have been hydrothermally silicified, seawater may have had a distinct Si isotope signature, or the mechanisms of Si enrichment may not have been the same as for Archean TTG sources. Finally, a limited, though detectable, influence of pre-existing crust is nevertheless visible in these magmas through zircon inheritance, which could also account for some of the low Si isotope signatures observed. Our study also provides an alternative explanation for the apparent homogenization of Si isotope signatures in the upper-continental crust over time, since Proterozoic TTGs can exhibit relatively low Si isotope signatures compared to their Archean equivalents. Published: 2026-04-26T22:21:34+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.555 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Syro Lacerda; Martin Guitreau; Leonardo Gonçalves; Hugo Moreira; Cristiane C. Gonçalves; Stéphany Rodrigues Lopes; Sandro Mauri; Marco Aurélio Pinheiro; Cassiano Costa e Castro; Régis Doucelance

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.04.026

Relevance: 0.555 (consider)

Tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite (TTGs) are silica-rich plutonic rocks that correspond to the dominant lithologies of Archean basements. Understanding their formation is essential to unravel the early Earth tectonic regime and constrain continental crust formation and evolution. Archean TTGs commonly exhibit heavy-silicon isotope compositions attributed to ocean-derived silica in their sources, thereby indicating an interaction with the surface. The sedimentary record shows that the dissolved silica in seawater (DSi) has decreased secularly from the Archean onward. Despite the general disappearance of TTGs after the Archean, some granitoids of TTG affinity can still be found in Paleoproterozoic crustal domains that are tectonically well-constrained. Comparing the geochemical characteristics of Archean and Proterozoic TTGs, as well as their sources, therefore appears important for feeding debates on the evolution of continental crust and tectonic settings. To shed new light on these questions, the present study examines the magmatic record of three Paleoproterozoic TTG units in the Mineiro Belt (Brazil) by focusing on whole-rock petrography and geochemistry alongside zircon U-Pb geochronology, elemental, and isotopic (Hf and Si) geochemistry. These new data indicate episodic formation of TTG magmas during the Rhyacian (2.15–2.10 Ga), mainly driven by melting of Rb- and Ba-enriched low-K mafic sources with variably long crustal residence times (∼0.2–0.8 Ga). Studied Paleoproterozoic TTGs exhibit lower silicon isotopic signatures than their Archean counterparts. This difference is likely linked to variations in source pre-melting histories, such that they may not have been hydrothermally silicified, seawater may have had a distinct Si isotope signature, or the mechanisms of Si enrichment may not have been the same as for Archean TTG sources. Finally, a limited, though detectable, influence of pre-existing crust is nevertheless visible in these magmas through zircon inheritance, which could also account for some of the low Si isotope signatures observed. Our study also provides an alternative explanation for the apparent homogenization of Si isotope signatures in the upper-continental crust over time, since Proterozoic TTGs can exhibit relatively low Si isotope signatures compared to their Archean equivalents.

item.title: Spatiotemporal monitoring of serpentinization in peridotite: mineral replacement, volume change and hydrogen production
item.dc:creator: Zhao Xia
D.Nicolas Espinoza
Carl Steefel
Michael Manga
Mengsu Hu
item.description: The growing demand for hydrogen necessitates innovative production strategies beyond conventional fossil fuel-derived methods. Stimulated geologic hydrogen generation through serpentinization of ultramafic rock is a promising alternative. However, the feedback mechanisms between fluid transport, mineral reactions, and rock deformation that govern the kinetics and evolution of serpentinization remain poorly constrained. This study investigates hydrogen yields and mineral phase transformations during laboratory serpentinization of peridotite. Experiments conducted at 90 °C and 0.1 MPa, and 200 °C and 6 MPa, show hydrogen yields of 0.5 to 0.9 μmol/g rock and 20.8 to 31.2 μmol/g rock after 30 days, respectively. High-resolution X-ray computed tomography captures the spatiotemporal (4D) evolution of serpentine vein networks during serpentinization, revealing serpentine growth primarily along pre-existing veins and sustaining fluid-mineral interactions through submicron vein permeability. Quantitative volume analyses show that serpentine volume fraction increases by 6.7 ± 0.93 to 10.2 ± 1.42 vol% from an initial value of 38 to 39 vol%, resulting in a volumetric strain of + 0.005 ± 0.00025 to + 0.006 ± 0.00012 (positive for volume increase) under zero effective stress. These results indicate that serpentine vein growth during serpentinization is primarily driven by mineral replacement at olivine-serpentine interfaces rather than by stress-induced fracturing followed by mineral infill, with fluid transport playing an important role in reaction progression. Overall, the findings highlight serpentinization as a coupled hydro-thermo-chemo-mechanical process that can occur over short timescales and at relatively low temperatures in peridotite, features that are desirable for scalable geologic hydrogen stimulation and production. Published: 2026-04-28T23:13:30+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.547 (ignore)

Authors: Zhao Xia; D.Nicolas Espinoza; Carl Steefel; Michael Manga; Mengsu Hu

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.04.029

Relevance: 0.547 (ignore)

The growing demand for hydrogen necessitates innovative production strategies beyond conventional fossil fuel-derived methods. Stimulated geologic hydrogen generation through serpentinization of ultramafic rock is a promising alternative. However, the feedback mechanisms between fluid transport, mineral reactions, and rock deformation that govern the kinetics and evolution of serpentinization remain poorly constrained. This study investigates hydrogen yields and mineral phase transformations during laboratory serpentinization of peridotite. Experiments conducted at 90 °C and 0.1 MPa, and 200 °C and 6 MPa, show hydrogen yields of 0.5 to 0.9 μmol/g rock and 20.8 to 31.2 μmol/g rock after 30 days, respectively. High-resolution X-ray computed tomography captures the spatiotemporal (4D) evolution of serpentine vein networks during serpentinization, revealing serpentine growth primarily along pre-existing veins and sustaining fluid-mineral interactions through submicron vein permeability. Quantitative volume analyses show that serpentine volume fraction increases by 6.7 ± 0.93 to 10.2 ± 1.42 vol% from an initial value of 38 to 39 vol%, resulting in a volumetric strain of + 0.005 ± 0.00025 to + 0.006 ± 0.00012 (positive for volume increase) under zero effective stress. These results indicate that serpentine vein growth during serpentinization is primarily driven by mineral replacement at olivine-serpentine interfaces rather than by stress-induced fracturing followed by mineral infill, with fluid transport playing an important role in reaction progression. Overall, the findings highlight serpentinization as a coupled hydro-thermo-chemo-mechanical process that can occur over short timescales and at relatively low temperatures in peridotite, features that are desirable for scalable geologic hydrogen stimulation and production.

item.title: Viscous drainage and dripping of eclogitized lower crust in the eastern Pamir imaged by geophysical data
item.dc:creator: Gang Wen
Yixian Xu
Bo Yang
Xiubin Lin
Xuewei Bao
Runyuan Zeng
Yi Zhang
item.description: Understanding whether and how dense rocks in continental crust are recycled back to mantle depths is crucial to deciphering the evolution of the Earth. Viscous drainage of the eclogitic layer in the lower thickened crust has been proposed as a mechanism that predates plate tectonics, but it has not been confirmed by observations. Specifically, it remains unclear whether this mechanism operated during orogenesis driven by modern plate tectonics. The Pamir Plateau lies at the western end of the active Himalayan-Tibetan orogen in the India-Asia collision zone. Here, we present a three-dimensional resistivity model constructed by inverting magnetotelluric data from the eastern Pamir and its surroundings. The mid-crustal conductors are interpreted as trapped aqueous fluids from eclogitization reactions in the lower thickened crust. Three-dimensional resistivity modeling supports the hypothesis of a nearly vertical collision mechanism between the East Pamir crust and the Tarim crust. In addition, the resistivity and velocity structures, as well as the characteristics of intermediate-depth earthquakes, can be well explained by viscous drainage followed by dripping of the eclogitic layer based on the petrophysical properties and rheological behavior. We argue that viscous drainage and dripping have initiated and are ongoing in the eastern Pamir, potentially representing the early-stage of stabilization in the orogen. Published: 2026-04-28T02:58:29+00:00 Venue: Science China Earth Sciences Score: 0.545 (ignore)
